text:
Chris Osei: Need a decision today on the publication criteria for “Known Issues” so we can lock PM-913 + unblock Docs PRs. Specifically: (1) confidence threshold to call something a known issue vs “investigating”, (2) workaround validation bar, (3) who owns updates/expiry. Draft is in PM-913: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/PM-913

Dev Patel: +1. Support keeps getting stuck in limbo where we *know* it’s real but Engineering doesn’t want us to publish until there’s an RCA. We need a middle ground.

Elliot Price: From docs POV: we can publish without RCA, but we need clear labeling and update responsibilities. Also we need to avoid anything customer-identifying. (We’ve had drafts with account IDs / exact tenant names—those can’t ship.)

Rachel Kim: Enterprise angle: customers don’t care if we have RCA yet, they care if the workaround is safe + the comms are consistent. But we can’t publish “try random knobs” and then eat it later.

Sean Gallagher: SRE request: don’t publish “known issue” unless there’s at least one of:
- confirmed telemetry signature (dashboards/alerts)
- internal repro
- multiple independent customer reports with matching request IDs + symptom pattern
If it’s “one customer says X” it’s not “known”, it’s “investigating”.

Dev Patel: Works. Can we define it as:
A) “Known issue” = we have high confidence it’s a real platform behavior + we have a workaround or mitigation steps that do not make things worse
B) “Investigating” = we’ve seen reports but no confirmed signal/repro
And we publish only A externally.

Priya Natarajan: Platform agrees with publish-only-A. For A, we should require an Eng/SRE reviewer, not just Support.

Owen Phillips: +1 reviewer requirement. We also need to state “When to escalate” explicitly so Support doesn’t keep re-triaging the same thing. That section has been missing in a bunch of drafts.

Wei Chen: Proposal for confidence threshold (so Support isn’t blocked on “RCA”):
- “Known issue” if *any* of:
  1) 3+ tickets in 14 days with same symptom pattern (region/model/tier tagged)
  2) 1 enterprise P0/P1 with Eng/SRE confirmation (telemetry or repro)
  3) internal incident or degradation event where comms already went out
- “Investigating” otherwise (internal-only page or keep in SUP ticket)
Does that satisfy SRE?

Sean Gallagher: Mostly. I’d tweak #1 to “3+ tickets OR 2+ distinct customers” because sometimes one big customer spams multiple tickets.

Jasmine Liu: Also add a fast path: if Support is getting repeat tickets *and* there’s a low-risk workaround that reduces impact, we should publish after reviewer sign-off even if the count is low.

Chris Osei: So: known issue requires confirmation + reviewer sign-off, but count can trigger the “we should go confirm this now” workflow.

Sofia Mendes: Legal/contract language: do not use “SLA breach” wording in KB articles. Use “service degradation” / “elevated errors” / “increased latency”. Also avoid promising timelines (“will be fixed by Friday”). Use “we’re working on a fix” + “next update by …” only in customer comms, not evergreen KB.

Kira Thompson: On workaround validation bar: for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private it’s different.
- Hosted: workaround must be client-side or request-level; cannot instruct customers to change infra we control.
- Dedicated: config changes allowed, but needs a rollback step + “expected outcome” + “risk”.
- Private: any cluster/installer action must be validated by Private Deployments/Platform; include what logs to collect before/after.

Dev Patel: +1. Can we codify “validated workaround” as:
- tested by Redwood (repro in staging OR at least one verified customer confirmation)
- reviewed by the owning Eng team (SRE/Platform/Runtime)
- includes: steps, expected result, rollback, and “when to escalate”

Priya Natarajan: For Platform-owned issues (SSO/audit logs/quota), reviewer is Platform oncall or named DRI; for routing/fallback, Mina’s team; for runtime perf, runtime oncall.

Sean Gallagher: And for anything that touches incident processes (regional degradation / widespread 5xx), add a line: “If you suspect broad impact, follow incident comms/status page guidance” + link. Otherwise we get docs used as substitute for status page.

Elliot Price: We can implement a template block in the KB:
- Status: Active | Monitoring | Resolved
- Last updated: date
- Affected: regions/models/tiers
- Workaround (validated)
- When to escalate
- Internal notes (NOT published)
And for “Resolved”, we keep it for 30 days then archive.

Rachel Kim: Who owns updates? I need this to not rot. Customers will screenshot stale KB.

Dev Patel: Suggest:
- Support owns the article (primary) + keeps “last updated” current
- Eng reviewer owns technical accuracy + must sign off on any material change
- If there’s a fix shipped, Eng DRI pings Support/Docs to update status + remove workaround if no longer needed

Chris Osei: That maps to a RACI. Also we can set an SLA for *updates*:
- Active known issues: review every 7 days minimum (even if “no change”) and bump last-updated
- Monitoring: review every 14 days
- Resolved: mark resolved + add fix version/date, then archive after 30 days

Sean Gallagher: 7 days is fine as a minimum. If it’s tied to an incident, updates will be more frequent via comms anyway.

Sofia Mendes: One more: include a disclaimer that workarounds are temporary and may vary by environment, esp Dedicated/Private.

Elliot Price: Decision check (so I can comment on PR-2841 review guidance):
1) We only publish external “Known Issues” when confidence is high (telemetry/repro/confirmed pattern) AND there is a validated workaround/mitigation.
2) “Investigating” stays internal (Support-only notes / SUP ticket), not in public KB.
3) Workaround must be reviewed by owning Eng/SRE + include steps, expected outcome, rollback, and “when to escalate”.
4) Ownership: Support DRI maintains; Eng DRI signs off; weekly/biweekly review cadence by status.
If yes, I’ll bake this into the publication checklist + template.

Jasmine Liu: Yes from CS. :thumbsup:

Dev Patel: Yes. This is exactly what Support needs.

Sean Gallagher: Yes with the “2+ distinct customers” tweak noted. :thumbsup:

Priya Natarajan: Yes. Please make sure the reviewer list in the playbook matches real oncall rotations (don’t name a team alias that doesn’t page anyone).

Chris Osei: Locked. I’ll update PM-913 with: “Known issue = high confidence + validated workaround + reviewer sign-off; investigating = internal-only.” Also adding the update cadence + RACI.

Wei Chen: I’ll update the KB template to include “Validated workaround” and “When to escalate” as required headings.

Elliot Price: Great. I’ll post this decision into the Docs thread + unblock PR-2841 review checklist.

Talia Benson: hey all — need final sign-off today on the Support SLA policy language + how it maps to tiering/pricing packaging. this is the last blocker for PM-912 + docs PR.

Talia Benson: Confluence draft (internal): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/support-sla-policy-by-customer-tier

Talia Benson: ask:
1) confirm tier names + definitions match pricing/packaging (Self-Serve / Dedicated / Private / Enterprise)
2) confirm update cadence wording + after-hours expectations won’t conflict w/ on-call + contracts
3) confirm escalation triggers and “Waiting on Customer” handling

Chris Osei: +1. also need someone to sanity check that “Enterprise” is a *Support tier* and not a product SKU. we’ve had Sales call everything enterprise.

Dev Patel: from Support side language is mostly good. main concern: the current draft says “Enterprise: updates every 30 min for P0” — we can do it during coverage, but after-hours we need explicit “best effort” + bridge-to-incidents path.

Rachel Kim: agree. for a lot of enterprise contracts we promise response times in business hours + “commercially reasonable efforts” after-hours unless they’ve purchased premium support. draft should reflect that nuance.

Sofia Mendes: Legal here — please avoid absolute language like “guarantee” and “will” outside the contractual SLA table. recommend:
- “target” for operational goals
- “subject to Support Hours” wherever applicable
- define “Support Hours” once (e.g., 24x5) and reference

Sean Gallagher: SRE: I’m mostly looking at the “escalate to SRE” thresholds. draft currently says “page SRE for P1 if >5% error rate for 10 min”. we should tie to customer impact + known alerting thresholds.

Sean Gallagher: suggest: “page SRE when you have (a) suspected platform/regional incident OR (b) multi-customer impact OR (c) error rate/latency thresholds consistent w/ SLO alerting”. keep the exact numbers in the SRE runbook, not the SLA doc.

Rafael Mendes: +1. hardcoding numbers in this doc will drift from PagerDuty alert thresholds.

Priya Natarajan: Platform: on tier definitions — Dedicated vs Private is clear. “Enterprise” overlaps: some Private customers are enterprise, but the deployment mode dictates escalation path/ownership. we should ensure the SLA doc doesn’t imply different *engineering* obligations by “Enterprise” label.

Talia Benson: ok. proposal:
- keep tiers as Support packages: Self-Serve, Dedicated, Private, Enterprise
- add a note: “Enterprise is a commercial Support tier that can apply to Hosted/Dedicated/Private depending on contract”

Chris Osei: that works for packaging. can you also add “Dedicated = reserved capacity contract” and “Private = customer-managed networking / on-prem optional” in the definitions section so there’s no ambiguity.

Avery Johnson: Sales POV: yes please. we need a one-liner we can paste into quoting notes. also: credits language — draft currently says “credits considered for P0 only.” is that still accurate?

Jasmine Liu: credits: keep it “case-by-case” and reference exec escalation protocol. we don’t want a blanket rule in a broadly referenced doc.

Dev Patel: on Waiting on Customer: we need explicit “SLA clock pauses when ticket is Waiting on Customer *and* we’ve requested specific artifacts.” otherwise we’ll get noise.

Sofia Mendes: careful: “pause” implies contractual SLA suspension. safer wording:
- “Support response targets do not apply while we are awaiting requested information from Customer.”
- define what qualifies as “requested information” (e.g., request IDs, timestamps, region/model).

Rachel Kim: +1, and we should say we’ll still monitor if it’s an ongoing incident (i.e., if it’s not actually blocked on them).

Sean Gallagher: on update cadence: if there’s an active incident bridge (#incidents), updates should be driven by incident commander, not Support. doc should say Support can relay, but comms ownership sits w/ IC/comms lead.

Dev Patel: yup. maybe: “For P0 incidents, Support will provide customer updates at the stated cadence OR mirror cadence from the active incident bridge/status page, whichever is more frequent during Support Hours.”

Rafael Mendes: slight tweak: “whichever is less confusing” lol. but yes, tie it to incident comms alignment doc.

Talia Benson: ack. adding link to comms alignment doc + sentence: “If an Incident is declared, customer-facing cadence follows the Incident Comms plan; Support ensures customer thread is updated accordingly.”

Priya Natarajan: also ensure escalation trigger for Platform-owned issues is explicit (SSO/audit logs/quota/routing). we’ve had tickets bounced.

Dev Patel: the playbook has that routing. but ok to include short “ownership quick map” section or at least link it.

Chris Osei: pls keep SLA doc lean; link to escalation paths page instead of duplicating contacts.

Talia Benson: ok, SLA doc will link to:
- escalation paths + contacts
- severity matrix
- executive escalation protocol

Sofia Mendes: another legal nit: in the SLA table, avoid “24/7” unless we define whether it’s follow-the-sun or on-call coverage. suggest: “After-hours coverage: on-call for P0 incidents (best effort) for Dedicated/Private/Enterprise as contracted.”

Rachel Kim: and we should be explicit that Self-Serve has no after-hours paging. (support will pick up next Support Hours)

Sean Gallagher: +1, otherwise people will page on-call for a self-serve 429.

Dev Patel: for Self-Serve, we should also clarify “updates” aren’t guaranteed; it’s async email support.

Avery Johnson: packaging question: are we ok with “Dedicated” having faster first response than “Enterprise Hosted”? some customers pay enterprise but still on Hosted.

Chris Osei: that’s why “Enterprise” needs to override by contract. we can phrase table as “minimum targets by tier; Enterprise contracts may supersede.”

Jasmine Liu: yep. keep the published table as the default. exceptions handled via contract / CS notes.

Talia Benson: cool. last check — anyone object to:
- P0 Enterprise: 15 min first response (Support Hours), 30 min updates (Support Hours)
- after-hours: on-call for P0 (best effort), hourly updates if bridge active + material change

Sean Gallagher: ok if “hourly updates” is phrased as “at least hourly when materially new info is available” — we shouldn’t force empty updates at 3am.

Dev Patel: +1. “at least hourly OR upon mitigation/ETA change.”

Sofia Mendes: +1. avoid “empty updates” obligation.

Priya Natarajan: fine by Platform.

Rachel Kim: fine by CS.

title: #product: 1772001234
preview:
Marta Ruiz: Quick sync — product decision needed on convo API shape. Customer asks: some want simple `prompt` param, others expect `messages` array with roles (system/user/assistant). We need launch criteria and scope. Thoughts?
Mike Chen: my 2c: canonical API should be `messages` array. It's explicit, maps to chat models and tool calling. Keep a `prompt` alias in SDKs for ease but server canonicalizes to `messages`.
Priya Patel: Agree. Engineering concerns: telemetry for role-level token counts, model routing decisions, and legacy clients that send a single string. We'll need automatic conversion + warn header. Also impacts batching.
Jordan Blake: Devex ask: docs + migration guides first-class. Example snippets, quickstart & playground support. Also update TS SDK types and python.
Samira Ortiz: Role semantics matter — system should be immutable and high-trust. Recommend server-side enforcement: single `system` messages allowed at top, merges with preconfigured system prompts. That avoids customer confusion.
Marta Ruiz: Good. What about function/tool calling + structured output? Does `messages` cover it?
Mike Chen: Yes. We'll extend `messages` items with `name`/`function_call` hints. Example:
```
messages: [
  {role: "system", content: "You are a helpful assistant."},
  {role: "user", content: "Lookup user orders"},
]
```
and responses stream ops unchanged.
Priya Patel: Launch criteria proposal:
- server canonicalizes `prompt` -> `messages`
- SDKs expose both shapes with deprecation warnings
- docs + migration guide
- telemetry: role token metrics + failure rates
- E2E tests with function-calling flows
Jordan Blake: Also need playground + codegen updates and sample datasets for evals.
Marta Ruiz: Priority: M1 (launch) = canonical messages + `prompt` alias + docs + SDK updates. M2 = stricter system enforcement + role-level policy settings + analytics dashboards. M3 = remove prompt alias (TBD by usage).
deploy-bot: scheduled deploy to staging for canary testing at 2025-12-15T09:00Z :rocket:
Marta Ruiz: OK. I'll draft RFC based on this and gate on telemetry threshold (degradation <1%) + docs ready. Thanks all :thumbsup:

title: #product: 1771698421
preview:
Marissa Cole: starting a thread to lock scope + success metrics for Suggestions beta in Console. we need alignment w/ Optimize team this week.

Marissa Cole: proposed beta scope = batching + caching suggestions only. UI = list + details drawer w/ “why”, estimated savings ($/day + %), latency deltas p50/p95, and risk label (low/med/high) w/ explanation + guardrails.

Aditya Rao: +1 on keeping it tight. please explicitly call out non-goals: quantization recs UI, auto-apply, and anything that reads like “guaranteed savings”.

Tessa Morgan: design can support the above. but if we include “model/quantization” in the nav or empty states, it will confuse people. suggest we reference it as “coming soon” only in docs, not in-product.

Dylan Brooks: eng-wise, we can ship this behind flag w/ suggestions API paging/sorting. agree that adding quantization to the view will blow up surface area + permissions + copy.

Chloe Martin: backend note: estimator v0 can output batching/caching deltas + confidence bands. quantization requires different inputs and longer validation.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: we should also be careful about “caching” meaning. prefix/KV caching vs response caching. beta should be prefix/KV + batching knobs only, right?

Aditya Rao: yes: v0 only controls we already expose in config (batch size, max queue delay, cache enablement policy) + deep links.

Marissa Cole: ok, writing this as: “Suggestions recommends changes to batching + prefix/KV caching configuration for a specific route+model deployment.”

Jonas Weber: +1. also: guardrail that we never recommend aggressive batching for low-latency routes (e.g., p95 SLO < 300ms). we should encode that as a “not eligible” reason.

Tessa Morgan: can we add a “not eligible” state that explains “we won’t recommend batching for routes with strict latency targets”? avoids users thinking telemetry is broken.

Logan Wright: telemetry side: we can distinguish “no data”, “insufficient traffic”, “not eligible due to guardrails”, and “compute delayed”. but please don’t invent 10 states :)

Marissa Cole: cool. success metrics: what are we using for beta success vs GA gates? initial thought:
1) engagement: impressions -> expand -> apply-click funnel
2) trust: dismiss reasons + “estimate feels wrong” feedback rate
3) system: API p95, compute lag, error rate
4) outcome: observed cost reduction where customers apply

Sean Gallagher: for GA we need explicit safety gates. suggest:
- no P0 incidents attributable to suggestions
- suggestions API p95 < 250ms at steady state
- compute job lag < 2h (or we show stale_as_of clearly)
- rollback plan tested

Dev Patel: support readiness gate too. we need canned responses + docs for “why does this not match billing” + “risk level means what?”. otherwise we drown.

Elliot Price: docs can cover disclaimers, estimator assumptions, and that estimates are directional. but we need final language locked (avoid “will save”).

Soojin Lee: from launch perspective, GA criteria needs a customer-facing framing too. “Beta” can be loose; “GA” implies reliability. i’d like a crisp checklist.

Aditya Rao: on estimation accuracy: we should set a gate like “median absolute % error under X” but that’s hard without ground truth.

Jonas Weber: we can validate on holdout historical windows. propose: for applied suggestions (internal dogfood + a few beta accounts), compare predicted savings vs realized token cost / req. accept if within ±20% median, ±40% p90. conservative.

Chloe Martin: realized savings attribution is messy bc customers change other stuff. but we can do “best effort” on controlled windows where only batching/caching changed.

George Mbatha: from UX research: comprehension is key. GA gate should include: in usability tests, >= 80% participants correctly interpret risk label as performance/reliability risk (not security), and understand estimates are not guaranteed.

Tessa Morgan: +1. we’ve had this confusion already in earlier mocks. need tooltip copy that says “risk of increased latency/timeouts/quality regression”.

Marissa Cole: ok. here’s a proposed split:
Beta success metrics (we monitor/learn):
- Suggestion expand rate >= 25% of impressions (per org w/ >= N impressions)

title: #product: 1770721205
preview:
Paula Mendes: Hey all — need alignment on canary-based rollout gates for runtime releases. Specifically what we mean by “auto-pause” in the Rollouts UX + release controller. Product expectation is: if perf-canary detects regression on a top model, rollout should stop automatically, with clear reason + next steps. Can we define exact semantics?

Marissa Cole: +1. We’ve been using “auto-pause” loosely in docs and it’s confusing. We should be explicit: pause future promotions vs rollback current. Also: which alert level gates? warning vs paging?

Connor O'Brien: From release-eng side: easiest/safest first cut is “pause promotions” only. i.e., release-controller holds at current stage (no new regions/tier expansions) until manual ack/override. Auto-rollback is a separate feature flag (and higher blast radius).

Noah Patel: +1. Canary signal is probabilistic + we’ve had baseline drift issues. Auto-rollback off a noisy signal is scary. Auto-pause gives time to validate + check attribution (queueing vs kernel vs infra).

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: please do NOT page on every gate. Gate decisions should be deterministic and low-noise. If we tie gating to warning alerts, we’ll freeze rollouts constantly.

Allison Grant: Agree. We should gate on “paging-level” conditions only (sustained regression, min sample size met, anti-flap). Warnings should show in Rollouts UI but not block by default.

Paula Mendes: OK so proposal: only paging-level perf-canary incidents trigger auto-pause by default. Warnings just annotate.

Rafael Mendes: Also: define who gets notified when auto-pause triggers. If it pauses a release at 2am, we need an oncall path.

Connor O'Brien: Mechanically we can have release-controller consume a “gate status” endpoint from perf-canary (or a computed metric in SLO toolkit). If gate=FAIL, it pauses and posts to #eng-releases + #eng-oncall (depending on severity) + creates an audit entry.

Marissa Cole: What does “paused” look like in Console? I want it to say something like “Paused by Perf Canary: llama-3.1-70b-instruct p95 +18% (baseline 420ms → 495ms), confidence high, region us-east, SKU H100” and a link to the dashboard + runbook.

Noah Patel: We can include that in the alert payload already (model, baseline, observed, window, minSamplesMet, links). For gating, we should standardize the payload fields so Console can render it.

Allison Grant: We should define the gate evaluation window. E.g. require regression condition for 10m AND >= N requests, not just a single spike. Otherwise you’ll pause on telemetry hiccups.

Vanessa Ortiz: Don’t forget fleet changes: sometimes we see p95 shift briefly during GPU maintenance / capacity rebalancing. If the gate auto-pauses, release-engineering will think “runtime broke” when it’s infra.

Noah Patel: That’s why we want attribution signals in the payload: queueing time vs runtime-only. If queueing is up and runtime-only stable, likely capacity/infra. Gate still might pause, but at least we label it “likely infra/queueing”.

Paula Mendes: Let’s nail down: does auto-pause happen if the regression is “likely infra”?

Sean Gallagher: I’d say yes initially, because from a customer perspective, regression is regression. But the runbook/escalation differs. Also, infra-caused should probably *not* block a runtime rollout if it’s clearly unrelated.

Connor O'Brien: We can split “hard fail” vs “soft fail” gates.
- hard fail: runtime-only latency regression OR tokens/sec drop with stable queueing -> likely runtime/kernel.
- soft fail: e2e regression dominated by queueing -> likely capacity.
Hard fail auto-pauses. Soft fail annotates + pings infra/SRE but doesn’t block promotions.

Allison Grant: That’s consistent with the paging hygiene direction. If you pause on soft fail, you’re mixing two different control loops.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Soft fail should create a ticket / notify #eng-infra, but not gate releases.

Marissa Cole: Product can live with that as long as it’s clear in UI: “Canary detected queueing-driven regression (non-gating)”. The “auto-pause” term should map to hard fail only.

Paula Mendes: Great. Now: do we gate only on “top models” or any model in canary coverage?

Noah Patel: Please top models only at first. Otherwise we’ll block on long-tail models with higher variance. We already classify top vs high-risk vs long-tail.

Allison Grant: Also important: region/SKU specificity. If EU-West has a blip, don’t pause a global rollout in US-East. Gate key should include (model, region, SKU, tier?) with sane defaults.

Connor O'Brien: Implementation thought: release stages already target specific region/tier. Gate evaluation should be scoped to the stage target. So if we’re rolling us-east hosted, only consider canary signals for us-east hosted.

Marissa Cole: That’s perfect. UX-wise we can show “Gate status: PASS/FAIL” per stage.

Paula Mendes: Another semantics Q: when auto-pause triggers, do we just stop *future* promotions, or also stop the canary rollout itself (like stop traffic shifting)?

Connor O'Brien: Release-controller can stop additional promotions, but if you’re mid-shift (e.g., 10%→25%), pausing should freeze at current percentage. It shouldn’t revert the already-shifted traffic unless we explicitly “rollback”.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Freeze is safer. Rollback should be an explicit action with human confirmation (at least until we’re very confident).

Noah Patel: Canary itself continues running regardless. And we should keep emitting the regression signal so we can see if it clears.

Allison Grant: Add a “cooldown” concept: once paused, don’t auto-unpause immediately on the first green datapoint. Require X minutes of PASS before suggesting resume.

Paula Mendes: So “auto-pause” is automatic; “auto-resume” is NOT automatic, but we can surface a “gate looks healthy” suggestion.

Marissa Cole: Yes. Also add an override button: “Proceed anyway” with required reason text + audit log. (Enterprise customers will ask for change management trail.)

Rafael Mendes: Strong +1 on audit log. Also ping oncall if someone overrides a FAIL gate outside business hours.

Vanessa Ortiz: One more: telemetry pipeline delays. If metrics are delayed/stale, we should not pause. Otherwise we’ll pause because we can’t see data.

Noah Patel: We can include “data_freshness_seconds” in gate evaluation and fail-open if stale (i.e., do not gate). But still warn: “Gate data stale.”

Allison Grant: “Fail-open” for gating makes sense. But we should create a separate alert for “canary blind” so we don’t ship with no signal.

Paula Mendes: Summarizing decisions:
1) Default gating only on hard-fail perf regressions for top models.
2) Auto-pause = freeze rollout promotions (and hold traffic shift at current %) for the affected stage.
3) No auto-rollback in v1.
4) Soft-fail (queueing/capacity-driven) does not gate; annotate + notify infra/SRE.
5) Fail-open if data stale; separate “canary blind” warning.
6) Manual resume + override w/ reason + audit.
Sound right?

Connor O'Brien: +1. I’ll update PM-642 with these semantics and draft the gate contract (fields + endpoint) for release-controller.

Marissa Cole: I’ll spec the Console wording/states (Paused by canary vs paused manually vs paused by policy) and the “Proceed anyway” flow.

Noah Patel: I’ll sync with Logan/Nadia on what we can reliably provide in payload (runtime-only vs e2e split, minSamplesMet, confidence).

Sean Gallagher: Please route the auto-pause notification to #eng-releases and #eng-oncall-runtime (not generic oncall) for hard fail. For soft fail: #eng-infra + #eng-sre.

Allison Grant: Can we avoid new channels in v1 and just use the existing incident-bot routing? But yes, we need deterministic routing rules.

Paula Mendes: Cool. I’ll send a quick write-up after this thread + schedule 30min to review the updated UX strings and the gate contract.

title: #product: 1770243102
preview:
Chris Osei: flagging a caveat for the Q1 pricing exp readout: meter fixes overlap w/ the experiment window. we need to be explicit in the doc + in the charts or we’re going to argue about conversion/margin deltas that are just metering.

Chris Osei: can we align on the segmentation rule? i’m proposing: pre-meter-fix vs post-meter-fix slices, plus exclude a small rollout buffer window.

Nikhil Sharma: yep. Variant B started 2026-01-22 for self-serve new signups. Meter changes started landing 2/06-ish (retry double-count fix + usage event idempotency tightening + region dimension backfill).

Nikhil Sharma: If we aggregate all of Jan+Feb we’re mixing apples/oranges.

Sana Farid: +1. From stats side, mixing measurement changes into outcome metrics breaks the causal story. Need a clean “measurement regime” dimension.

Sana Farid: Suggest we do:
- Primary analysis: only post-meter-fix (stable meter) and only users first exposed after the fix
- Secondary: full window but include meter_version controls + note discontinuity

Logan Wright: meter side: we have at least 3 separate “gotchas”:
1) ENG-4430 / PR-27463: fixed double-counting on server retry/fallback for streaming. That will reduce tokens billed for some traffic.
2) PR-27555: idempotency/dedupe improvements. That reduces duplicate usage events in certain partial failure paths.
3) PR-27541 + backfill: region dimension was missing for some events → those were getting defaulted in downstream agg. Backfill changes historical breakdowns (and some pricing if region-based multiplier applies).

Logan Wright: also PR-27518 shadow billing compare started earlier, but it wasn’t customer-facing. The “real invoice meter” changed when we flipped the gate (Finance-approved). We need to anchor on that date.

Nadia Rahman: concretely: “meter_version” for hosted tokens is `hosted_tokens_v1` vs `hosted_tokens_v1_1` (retry+streaming patch) + `v1_2` (dedupe key change). it’s not a public field but we can derive from the internal usage_events schema version + deploy tag.

Nadia Rahman: for analysis, easiest is to use `usage_event_schema_version` + `billing_calc_version` from the recon export (Snowflake). That already captures the flip.

Rishi Malhotra: for FP&A model + margin readout: we need to avoid interpreting lower “tokens billed” as “customers used less”. It’s just we stopped double counting.

Rishi Malhotra: So guardrail: any ARPU / GM charts must show both:
- raw tokens (actual work done)
- billed tokens (invoice-facing)
…and annotate when billed token definition changed.

Chris Osei: ok so for the readout:
1) add an “Instrumentation / Metering Changes” slide w/ a timeline
2) for key outcomes (conversion, retention, ARPU, GM): show pre/post and highlight discontinuity
3) when we do cohort-based retention: define cohorts by *exposure date* AND *meter regime*

Jordan Blake: for GTM: can we put a crisp disclaimer in the exec summary? like “meter fixes materially affect billed token volume starting Feb 6; conclusions about unit economics are based on post-fix data.” sales is going to ask “why did margin jump overnight?”

Aisha Bello: we should also include the alert/incident context. there was an elevated duplicate rate in late Jan during the backfill attempt, and we tuned it down after. that could create a weird spike if you’re looking at daily usage.

Aisha Bello: I can pull the exact date range for the duplicate-rate anomaly + recommend exclusion window (e.g., 2026-01-28 to 2026-01-30).

title: #product: 1770171022
preview:
Chris Osei: Dropping a thread for Packaging Variants v3 open Qs ahead of exec review. Please reply w/ answers or “needs decision” so I can roll into the doc.

Chris Osei: Link to v3 draft (working) in Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/redwood/product-ops/packaging-variants-v3-working-draft (same as last week, new tab “Open Questions”).

Nikhil Sharma: +1. Biggest thing: we need to be explicit on what’s “billed tokens” vs “raw tokens” in the packaging copy. Meter fixes are changing some semantics.

Soojin Lee: Adding from PMM side: Sales keeps asking if we can say “cache hits are free” or “cache hits are discounted” and what the customer sees in usage exports.

Chris Osei: Cool. Here’s the Q list (please tag w/ #1 etc):
1) Free tier: do we hard-cap requests/tokens or allow overage at list?
2) Overage behavior: do we soft limit w/ alerts + allow, or block (HTTP 402/quota) when included usage is exceeded?
3) Plan limits: are limits per workspace, per API key, or per org? Any per-user concept?
4) Enterprise exceptions: do we allow custom overage rates, or only custom “included” bundles?
5) Model mix: do we keep a single blended token rate across models in a plan, or per-model multipliers?
6) Caching: how do we attribute tokens for cache hits in the bundle (0 billed? partial billed?)
7) Retries/fallback: do we ever bill for server-side retries? (and how do we message it)
8) Billing granularity: daily vs monthly included usage burn-down (what do we show in Console)
9) Credits: do promo credits apply to overage only, included only, or both?
10) Dispute window / refunds: do we need packaging-specific language or just MSA standard?

Jordan Blake: Re #2 (overage behavior): for self-serve we should not hard block unless fraud/abuse. Blocking causes churn + support load. Prefer soft limit + alert + “upgrade now” in Console.

Emily Stone: Finance angle: soft limit is fine but we need deterministic invoice outcome. If soft limit = unlimited, we need clear overage rates and no “surprise” usage definitions. Also month-end close freeze window — late events can cause “included” to shift unless we define a cutoff.

Rishi Malhotra: For #5 model mix: blended rate makes forecasting easy but can nuke margin if customers camp on large models. Suggest blended for Starter only; Pro/Business should be “base rate + per-model adders” or we enforce model gating by plan.

Nikhil Sharma: Agree. Also we’re about to expose `billed_tokens` vs `raw_tokens` + `cache_hit` in Usage API. If we do blended, it should key off billed tokens only.

Soojin Lee: Wait — how do we explain per-model adders without sounding like “gotcha pricing”? Our current positioning is “predictable.”

Jordan Blake: We can say “plan includes access to X models; premium models priced separately” (like add-on). AEs can sell it.

Sana Farid: On #1 free tier: experiments show hard caps reduce abuse but also lower activation for legit devs. If we keep hard caps, we should set them high enough + add a “verify email + add card” step to extend.

Chris Osei: Can someone propose actual numbers for free tier cap (tokens + requests) for v3? Even if placeholders.

Rishi Malhotra: Placeholder proposal (needs exec): Free = 1M tokens/mo + 5k requests/mo, no overage (hard cap). Starter = 20M tokens/mo included + overage at list. Pro = 100M tokens/mo included + lower overage.

Jordan Blake: 1M tokens/mo free w/ no overage feels too stingy for AI-native devs. Also “no overage” = hard stop mid-demo.

Nikhil Sharma: Another option: Free includes small amount + allow overage only after card added (no invoice until threshold). But that’s more product work.

Emily Stone: If overage is allowed on Free with no card, we take on collections risk and revrec complexity. If card required for overage, cleaner.

Soojin Lee: Can we phrase it as “free credits” rather than “included tokens”? Then it’s less weird to require card to continue.

Chris Osei: Logging as decision point: Free tier = credits framing + card required for overage.

Sofia Mendes: Re #4 enterprise exceptions: legal/contracting preference is to keep a small set of constructs.
- OK: custom included bundle, custom committed spend, custom discount % on list.
- Risky: custom definitions of “token” / caching / retries per customer.
If we allow custom overage rates, we need a standard order form template w/ guardrails + clear “usage definition is per Redwood policy.”

Michael Grant: +1. Also for #10 dispute/refunds: I’d rather centralize in usage terms playbook. Packaging page can reference the standard dispute window + “good faith” investigation, but don’t invent plan-specific promises.

Jordan Blake: For #4, Sales will push for “custom overage” on big logos. But fine if we constrain it to discount off published overage rate.

Rishi Malhotra: That’s basically a discount. Works for margin modeling as long as we have approval matrix.

Chris Osei: Great, so #4 answer: enterprise can do discount off list, not bespoke rate card.

Nikhil Sharma: Re #6 caching: we need a clear rule:
- If cache hit returns previously computed tokens, we shouldn’t bill compute again.
But do we bill *any* tokens for the response? (some vendors bill “served tokens” even from cache).

Emily Stone: Billing from a revrec angle: either is fine as long as consistent + disclosed. BUT if we say “cache hits are free,” we need to ensure meter integrity (no double count) and be prepared for customer audits.

Soojin Lee: PMM preference: “Cache hits are discounted (or not billed) — shown separately in usage exports.” If we can show it in Console, it becomes a value prop.

Nikhil Sharma: Technically we can attribute `cache_hit=true` and set `billed_tokens=0` for the cached portion. Question is whether we want “partial” when prefix cached but completion generated.

Chris Osei: Can we align on: prefix cache hit => prefix tokens billed = 0; generated completion billed normally.

Nikhil Sharma: That’s how the implementation is trending. Need to confirm edge cases (streaming + tool calls) but directionally yes.

Sana Farid: If we switch cache billing mid-experiment window, it’ll bias outcomes. We should annotate “pre/post cache meter fix” in analysis.

Chris Osei: Noted (I’ll add a caveat slide).

Nikhil Sharma: Re #7 retries/fallback: policy should be “no double billing for server-side retries/fallback.” We should bill only once per idempotency key / request_id.

Jordan Blake: Please also make sure Support/AEs can say that in plain English.

Michael Grant: Wording: “You will not be charged more than once for the same request due to Redwood-initiated retries or fallback.” BUT leave carve-out for customer-initiated retries / new requests.

Emily Stone: + add “except where you submit a new request” so we’re not on the hook for client replays.

Soojin Lee: We should add a FAQ entry: “If my client retries after timeout, am I billed twice?” Answer: if you reuse idempotency key, we dedupe; if not, it’s a new request. (Engineering please confirm.)

Nikhil Sharma: Yes, idempotency key is the mechanism. We need to ensure SDKs default to it.

Chris Osei: Re #8 burn-down granularity: what’s the plan? daily included usage remaining?

Rishi Malhotra: For Finance, monthly is the invoice unit. For customers, daily burn-down + forecast is better UX.

Jordan Blake: Customers ask for daily exports all the time. Having daily burn-down in Console will reduce “surprise overage” escalation.

Emily Stone: As long as it’s labeled “estimated” until close. We should define a “usage data freshness” SLA.

Chris Osei: OK: show daily burn-down in Console, but make it clear it may lag; invoice based on month-end finalized ledger.

Sofia Mendes: Make sure any “estimate” language is consistent with contract. Don’t promise real-time.

Chris Osei: Re #9 credits: do credits apply to both included + overage? I assume credits offset overage only?

Emily Stone: Typically credits offset invoice total. If plan has included usage, invoice starts at $0 for included; overage is billable; credits can apply to remaining balance. But we should avoid “credits increasing included allowance” because then we have to track weird carryover semantics.

Rishi Malhotra: +1. Modeling assumes credits reduce cash/rev, not expand included. Simpler: credits apply post-metering to charges.

Jordan Blake: Works for Sales as long as we can position “credits reduce your bill.”

Chris Osei: Summarizing tentative answers for the doc:
- #1 Free tier: credits framing; hard cap; card required to continue/overage (decision needed on exact thresholds).
- #2 Overage: soft limit for paid plans (alerts + allow), no hard block except abuse.
- #3 Limits: per org/workspace; API key is not a billing boundary.
- #4 Enterprise exceptions: allowed = discount off published rates / custom included bundle; not allowed = bespoke usage definitions.
- #5 Model mix: blended for low tier; gating/add-ons or per-model multipliers for higher tiers (decision needed).
- #6 Caching: cached prefix billed_tokens=0; generated tokens billed; usage export shows cache_hit.
- #7 Retries/fallback: never double-bill for Redwood-initiated retries; idempotency key required for client retries.
- #8 Burn-down: daily estimate in Console; month-end finalized for invoice.
- #9 Credits: apply to charges post-metering (primarily overage), not as “extra included.”
- #10 Dispute/refunds: reference standard usage terms; no plan-specific promises.

Evelyn Hart: This is good. Two decisions for exec review: (a) free tier cap + card gating, (b) model gating vs blended. Can you tee those up as explicit options w/ pros/cons and margin impact?

Rishi Malhotra: I can add margin deltas for blended vs gating (esp on large model usage). Will post in the working draft in ~2 hrs.

Soojin Lee: I’ll draft customer-facing phrasing for caching + retries that doesn’t scare people. Will paste here for review.

Michael Grant: Send me the final copy before it goes to Sales enablement. Need to avoid absolute guarantees.

Chris Osei: Perfect. I’ll update the “Open Questions” tab and prep the exec pre-read. :thumbsup:
text:
Chris Osei: Dropping a thread for Packaging Variants v3 open Qs ahead of exec review. Please reply w/ answers or “needs decision” so I can roll into the doc.

Chris Osei: Link to v3 draft (working) in Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/redwood/product-ops/packaging-variants-v3-working-draft (same as last week, new tab “Open Questions”).

Nikhil Sharma: +1. Biggest thing: we need to be explicit on what’s “billed tokens” vs “raw tokens” in the packaging copy. Meter fixes are changing some semantics.

Soojin Lee: Adding from PMM side: Sales keeps asking if we can say “cache hits are free” or “cache hits are discounted” and what the customer sees in usage exports.

Chris Osei: Cool. Here’s the Q list (please tag w/ #1 etc):
1) Free tier: do we hard-cap requests/tokens or allow overage at list?
2) Overage behavior: do we soft limit w/ alerts + allow, or block (HTTP 402/quota) when included usage is exceeded?
3) Plan limits: are limits per workspace, per API key, or per org? Any per-user concept?
4) Enterprise exceptions: do we allow custom overage rates, or only custom “included” bundles?
5) Model mix: do we keep a single blended token rate across models in a plan, or per-model multipliers?
6) Caching: how do we attribute tokens for cache hits in the bundle (0 billed? partial billed?)
7) Retries/fallback: do we ever bill for server-side retries? (and how do we message it)
8) Billing granularity: daily vs monthly included usage burn-down (what do we show in Console)
9) Credits: do promo credits apply to overage only, included only, or both?
10) Dispute window / refunds: do we need packaging-specific language or just MSA standard?

Jordan Blake: Re #2 (overage behavior): for self-serve we should not hard block unless fraud/abuse. Blocking causes churn + support load. Prefer soft limit + alert + “upgrade now” in Console.

Emily Stone: Finance angle: soft limit is fine but we need deterministic invoice outcome. If soft limit = unlimited, we need clear overage rates and no “surprise” usage definitions. Also month-end close freeze window — late events can cause “included” to shift unless we define a cutoff.

Rishi Malhotra: For #5 model mix: blended rate makes forecasting easy but can nuke margin if customers camp on large models. Suggest blended for Starter only; Pro/Business should be “base rate + per-model adders” or we enforce model gating by plan.

Nikhil Sharma: Agree. Also we’re about to expose `billed_tokens` vs `raw_tokens` + `cache_hit` in Usage API. If we do blended, it should key off billed tokens only.

Soojin Lee: Wait — how do we explain per-model adders without sounding like “gotcha pricing”? Our current positioning is “predictable.”

Jordan Blake: We can say “plan includes access to X models; premium models priced separately” (like add-on). AEs can sell it.

Sana Farid: On #1 free tier: experiments show hard caps reduce abuse but also lower activation for legit devs. If we keep hard caps, we should set them high enough + add a “verify email + add card” step to extend.

Chris Osei: Can someone propose actual numbers for free tier cap (tokens + requests) for v3? Even if placeholders.

Rishi Malhotra: Placeholder proposal (needs exec): Free = 1M tokens/mo + 5k requests/mo, no overage (hard cap). Starter = 20M tokens/mo included + overage at list. Pro = 100M tokens/mo included + lower overage.

Jordan Blake: 1M tokens/mo free w/ no overage feels too stingy for AI-native devs. Also “no overage” = hard stop mid-demo.

Nikhil Sharma: Another option: Free includes small amount + allow overage only after card added (no invoice until threshold). But that’s more product work.

Emily Stone: If overage is allowed on Free with no card, we take on collections risk and revrec complexity. If card required for overage, cleaner.

Soojin Lee: Can we phrase it as “free credits” rather than “included tokens”? Then it’s less weird to require card to continue.

Chris Osei: Logging as decision point: Free tier = credits framing + card required for overage.

Sofia Mendes: Re #4 enterprise exceptions: legal/contracting preference is to keep a small set of constructs.
- OK: custom included bundle, custom committed spend, custom discount % on list.
- Risky: custom definitions of “token” / caching / retries per customer.
If we allow custom overage rates, we need a standard order form template w/ guardrails + clear “usage definition is per Redwood policy.”

Michael Grant: +1. Also for #10 dispute/refunds: I’d rather centralize in usage terms playbook. Packaging page can reference the standard dispute window + “good faith” investigation, but don’t invent plan-specific promises.

Jordan Blake: For #4, Sales will push for “custom overage” on big logos. But fine if we constrain it to discount off published overage rate.

Rishi Malhotra: That’s basically a discount. Works for margin modeling as long as we have approval matrix.

Chris Osei: Great, so #4 answer: enterprise can do discount off list, not bespoke rate card.

Nikhil Sharma: Re #6 caching: we need a clear rule:
- If cache hit returns previously computed tokens, we shouldn’t bill compute again.
But do we bill *any* tokens for the response? (some vendors bill “served tokens” even from cache).

Emily Stone: Billing from a revrec angle: either is fine as long as consistent + disclosed. BUT if we say “cache hits are free,” we need to ensure meter integrity (no double count) and be prepared for customer audits.

Soojin Lee: PMM preference: “Cache hits are discounted (or not billed) — shown separately in usage exports.” If we can show it in Console, it becomes a value prop.

Nikhil Sharma: Technically we can attribute `cache_hit=true` and set `billed_tokens=0` for the cached portion. Question is whether we want “partial” when prefix cached but completion generated.

Chris Osei: Can we align on: prefix cache hit => prefix tokens billed = 0; generated completion billed normally.

Nikhil Sharma: That’s how the implementation is trending. Need to confirm edge cases (streaming + tool calls) but directionally yes.

Sana Farid: If we switch cache billing mid-experiment window, it’ll bias outcomes. We should annotate “pre/post cache meter fix” in analysis.

Chris Osei: Noted (I’ll add a caveat slide).

Nikhil Sharma: Re #7 retries/fallback: policy should be “no double billing for server-side retries/fallback.” We should bill only once per idempotency key / request_id.

Jordan Blake: Please also make sure Support/AEs can say that in plain English.

Michael Grant: Wording: “You will not be charged more than once for the same request due to Redwood-initiated retries or fallback.” BUT leave carve-out for customer-initiated retries / new requests.

Emily Stone: + add “except where you submit a new request” so we’re not on the hook for client replays.

Soojin Lee: We should add a FAQ entry: “If my client retries after timeout, am I billed twice?” Answer: if you reuse idempotency key, we dedupe; if not, it’s a new request. (Engineering please confirm.)

Nikhil Sharma: Yes, idempotency key is the mechanism. We need to ensure SDKs default to it.

Chris Osei: Re #8 burn-down granularity: what’s the plan? daily included usage remaining?

Rishi Malhotra: For Finance, monthly is the invoice unit. For customers, daily burn-down + forecast is better UX.

Jordan Blake: Customers ask for daily exports all the time. Having daily burn-down in Console will reduce “surprise overage” escalation.

Emily Stone: As long as it’s labeled “estimated” until close. We should define a “usage data freshness” SLA.

Chris Osei: OK: show daily burn-down in Console, but make it clear it may lag; invoice based on month-end finalized ledger.

Sofia Mendes: Make sure any “estimate” language is consistent with contract. Don’t promise real-time.

Chris Osei: Re #9 credits: do credits apply to both included + overage? I assume credits offset overage only?

Emily Stone: Typically credits offset invoice total. If plan has included usage, invoice starts at $0 for included; overage is billable; credits can apply to remaining balance. But we should avoid “credits increasing included allowance” because then we have to track weird carryover semantics.

Rishi Malhotra: +1. Modeling assumes credits reduce cash/rev, not expand included. Simpler: credits apply post-metering to charges.

Jordan Blake: Works for Sales as long as we can position “credits reduce your bill.”

Chris Osei: Summarizing tentative answers for the doc:
- #1 Free tier: credits framing; hard cap; card required to continue/overage (decision needed on exact thresholds).
- #2 Overage: soft limit for paid plans (alerts + allow), no hard block except abuse.
- #3 Limits: per org/workspace; API key is not a billing boundary.
- #4 Enterprise exceptions: allowed = discount off published rates / custom included bundle; not allowed = bespoke usage definitions.
- #5 Model mix: blended for low tier; gating/add-ons or per-model multipliers for higher tiers (decision needed).
- #6 Caching: cached prefix billed_tokens=0; generated tokens billed; usage export shows cache_hit.
- #7 Retries/fallback: never double-bill for Redwood-initiated retries; idempotency key required for client retries.
- #8 Burn-down: daily estimate in Console; month-end finalized for invoice.
- #9 Credits: apply to charges post-metering (primarily overage), not as “extra included.”
- #10 Dispute/refunds: reference standard usage terms; no plan-specific promises.

Evelyn Hart: This is good. Two decisions for exec review: (a) free tier cap + card gating, (b) model gating vs blended. Can you tee those up as explicit options w/ pros/cons and margin impact?

Rishi Malhotra: I can add margin deltas for blended vs gating (esp on large model usage). Will post in the working draft in ~2 hrs.

Soojin Lee: I’ll draft customer-facing phrasing for caching + retries that doesn’t scare people. Will paste here for review.

Michael Grant: Send me the final copy before it goes to Sales enablement. Need to avoid absolute guarantees.

Chris Osei: Perfect. I’ll update the “Open Questions” tab and prep the exec pre-read. :thumbsup:

title: #product: 1770132280
preview:
Chris Osei: Heads up: the Variant B pricing experiment is getting *really* noisy after this week’s meter fixes. We need a decision today: stop vs continue (or pause enrollment) so we don’t publish a misleading readout. Current dashboard is showing a step-change in billed_tokens on Feb 12–13 and conversion looks like it “improved” overnight.

Nikhil Sharma: Yeah, I’m looking at it now. The step-change lines up exactly with the usage event idempotency + streaming retry fix rollout. It’s not a user behavior change.

Sana Farid: +1. If the measurement changed mid-flight, the causal story is broken unless we segment pre/post and re-baseline. Otherwise the lift is basically “we stopped double counting.”

Rishi Malhotra: From Finance lens, we can’t use this to justify packaging decisions if the unit economics inputs moved mid-experiment. Margin model uses billed_tokens and cache_hit attribution — both are shifting.

Chris Osei: The open question: do we (a) stop Variant B entirely, (b) keep running but freeze new exposure + analyze only clean window, or (c) extend experiment with a fresh ramp once meters stabilized.

Logan Wright: Quick context: Feb 12 rollout included:
- fix for double-counting on retry/fallback (esp streaming)
- dedupe/idempotency improvements
- some backfilled missing fields for cache_hit on a subset
So the “billed_tokens” time series discontinuity is expected. It should be *more correct* now.

Nadia Rahman: Important nuance: it’s more correct, but it’s not comparable to the earlier window. Also for a few hours we had partial deployment by region, so aggregates are extra weird. If you do anything, segment by `meter_version` (old/new) + region.

Jordan Blake: From GTM: we promised a decision for AE guidance early next week. If we stop now, we’ll be short on sample for enterprise-ish accounts. But I’d rather be late than ship a story customers will pick apart.

Evelyn Hart: What’s our stop criteria in the experiment doc? Did we already hit any hard guardrails (revenue leakage / churn signals)?

Chris Osei: Guardrails are fine. No spike in refunds/credits requests. The issue is statistical/measurement: conversion rate and ARPU are unstable because the denominator moved.

Sana Farid: If we want a defensible readout, we need:
1) freeze enrollment while meters settle (avoid mixing)
2) define “clean” measurement window post-fix (e.g., after full rollout + after late events catch up)
3) re-run power calc / expected time to detect
We can still keep existing cohort in their assigned plan to avoid customer thrash.

Nikhil Sharma: Agree. Pausing enrollment is easy (flag off for new). Keeping existing exposure avoids changing pricing on people mid-month.

Monica Patel: FYI SDK retry behavior changed slightly too (we’re aligning idempotency key usage in the TS client). That might affect request counts for a small subset if they were doing aggressive client retries. Another reason not to compare pre/post.

Rishi Malhotra: Also revrec: if we’re changing meters, we need clean documentation on the window used for analysis vs billing, otherwise we’ll get questions later when metrics don’t match invoice previews.

Chris Osei: Ok but what’s the decision? “Continue” vs “stop” is what leadership asked me to summarize.

Evelyn Hart: Proposal: continue experiment intent, but treat it as a restart. Pause new enrollments immediately, then restart enrollment once we have 72h of stable metering + consistent region coverage. Publish readout with explicit “pre-meter-fix data excluded.”

Jordan Blake: Works for me as long as we have a date. When do we expect stability?

Logan Wright: If no more rollouts, we can call it stable once:
- drift checks vs billing ledger are <0.5% for 3 consecutive days
- duplicate rate steady
- late event lag back under SLO
We can probably hit that by mid next week.

Nadia Rahman: + we should annotate the dashboard with a “meter fix boundary” so no one screenshots the step-change as a result.

Sana Farid: If we “restart,” we should also reset the analysis plan: primary metric is conversion-to-paid within 7 days; secondary ARPU at day-14; and margin overlays need the new billed_tokens definition. I can update the stop/continue memo.

Chris Osei: Concrete plan:
- Today: pause new exposure for Variant B (keep existing users on their assigned plan)
- Define clean window start = once meter_sanity dashboard green for 3 days
- Re-ramp enrollment next week, run for 2 weeks post-clean window
- Readout includes only post-clean window + cohorts tagged with meter_version=new
Does anyone object?

Rishi Malhotra: No objection. Please add a note that Finance will only sign off on conclusions using post-fix data and include the reconciliation export link.

Jordan Blake: No objection. Please send a quick note I can forward to Sales so they stop asking “why did ARPU jump yesterday.”

Nikhil Sharma: I’ll flip the experiment flag for new signups and leave existing assignments untouched. Chris, ping me the exact flag name you want paused (self-serve only vs all).

Chris Osei: Pause self-serve new first; keep sales-led accounts as-is for now (we’ll review individually). I’ll post in #sales-enablement too.

Evelyn Hart: Decision recorded: *pause new enrollment, continue as restart post-meter-stabilization*. Chris/Sana: update the readout doc with the caveat and new timeline.

Sana Farid: On it. I’ll also add a short section on why pre/post mixing invalidates the diff-in-diff assumption. (Keeping it non-academic.)

Nadia Rahman: Please make sure analysis uses `billed_tokens` not `raw_tokens` and that cache_hit attribution is consistent. We’ve seen folks accidentally mix those in Looker.

Logan Wright: I’ll drop a one-liner query snippet for filtering on `meter_version` + the rollout boundary timestamps. Also will add an annotation request to the dashboard PR.

Chris Osei: Great. Action items:
- Nikhil: pause self-serve enrollment (today)
- Logan/Nadia: confirm “clean window” criteria + boundary timestamps
- Sana: updated analysis plan + stop criteria re-baseline
- Chris: comms to Sales + exec update blurb

Jordan Blake: :thumbsup: thanks all. Just keep me looped on the new “decision date” so I can reset expectations.

title: #product: 1770050444
preview:
Chris Osei: rolling up a rollout checklist for pricing experiment Variant B. want to run it by folks before we flip anything. can we confirm: eligibility, flags, dashboards, comms?

Nikhil Sharma: yes pls. Variant B is the “new free tier + overage guardrail copy” right? + tweak to overage rate for self-serve?

Chris Osei: yep. Variant A was copy-only, Variant B is copy + packaging thresholds (included usage + overage behavior).

Sana Farid: reminder: if meters changed last week, we need explicit pre/post segmentation in the analysis. for rollout checklist: record meter_version and experiment cohort on every request.

Logan Wright: +1. from telemetry side we can tag `meter_version` + `pricing_experiment_variant`. PR-27533 adds cohort tagging but we need to ensure it’s wired on OpenAI-compat + SDK retries.

Monica Patel: SDK side: python/ts will pass idempotency keys by default on retries, but older clients may not. for the experiment cohort tagging, can we do it server-side based on account_id so it’s not client-dependent?

Nikhil Sharma: cohort assignment should be server-side (control plane) based on account + workspace. client should be unaware.

Chris Osei: ok draft checklist items (pls correct):
1) Eligibility:
- self-serve only (no sales-led contracts)
- Hosted API only (exclude Dedicated + Private)
- exclude accounts with custom rate cards / invoice terms
- exclude accounts with open billing disputes (Support tag)
- geo: start US/EU only?
2) Flags:
- LaunchDarkly: `pricing_exp_2026q1` + `pricing_exp_variant=B`
- ramp: 0% -> 5% (24h) -> 25% (48h) -> 50% if stable
- ensure holdout/control remains 50%
3) Telemetry:
- events: `pricing_plan_exposed`, `plan_id`, `variant`, `cohort_id`
- attach to usage events + billing ledger export (Finance recon)
4) Dashboards:
- conversion funnel, ARPU, gross margin proxy, churn/retention
- guardrail: support tickets, dispute rate, “usage export mismatch” cases
5) Comms:
- Sales/Support FAQ snippet + internal heads-up
- finance: invoice preview note for impacted accounts

Soojin Lee: on comms: if we change overage behavior, we need consistent copy in Console + docs + emails. otherwise Support gets crushed.

Jordan Blake: sales request: clear “what changed / who is affected / how to answer fairness questions”. esp billed vs raw tokens + caching.

Rishi Malhotra: finance request: for the ramp steps, can we get an “invoice preview” report daily for impacted cohort? need to validate revenue/margin deltas aren’t coming from meter drift.

Aisha Bello: dashboards: I can add an annotation stream when ramp changes (5%/25%/50%) so we can correlate with metrics + alerts. but need the exact flag key + timestamp.

Ethan Park: eligibility can be enforced at control plane: include rule “account.plan_type == SELF_SERVE” and “account.billing_profile == STANDARD”. we can also block if `billing_hold=true`.

Chris Osei: love it. quick questions:
- US/EU only: do we need region constraint for pricing? or just ensure region dimension is correct for margin attribution.

Logan Wright: pricing calc shouldn’t be region dependent but margin model is. we had the “missing region dimension” incident recently. i’d prefer we limit to accounts where region is stable + the region dimension is present (null-rate < threshold) for the first ramp.

Sana Farid: +1. for experimental validity: excluding accounts with missing region may bias, but for rollout safety it’s fine. just document that exclusion.

Nikhil Sharma: ok, include a “data quality eligibility”: only accounts with >= 99.5% non-null `region` on usage events in last 7d.

Chris Osei: adding.

Soojin Lee: also: console surfaces “included tokens” and “overage starts at”. need to ensure the UI strings are behind the same flag, otherwise ppl see mismatch vs invoice.

Monica Patel: and API/SDK usage endpoint needs to show the same fields (billed_tokens + cache_hit) so customers can reconcile. is PR-27485 already in prod?

Logan Wright: PR-27485 is staged, rolling out to prod this week. but experiment B shouldn’t depend on it being customer-visible—just helps support.

Support Bot: FYI Support queue label `billing-usage-discrepancy` currently at 14 open tickets (last 7d). Top issue: “invoice tokens don’t match usage export”.

Jordan Blake: that’s exactly why we need comms in advance. don’t want the experiment to look like it increased disputes when it’s just confusion.

Rishi Malhotra: on ramp: can we add stop criteria: dispute rate > X%, or gross margin delta outside expected band, or meter drift alerts firing.

Sana Farid: propose stop criteria for first 48h:
- dispute/support tickets in exposed cohort > control by +30% (normalized by active accounts)
- conversion drop > 10% vs control (new signups)
- revenue per active account change > +/-20% without corresponding usage shift
- meter drift alert triggered (ledger vs usage events) beyond threshold

Aisha Bello: we can pipe the drift alert signal into the experiment dashboard as a red banner.

Nikhil Sharma: ok.

Chris Osei: flag + cohort details: do we have final naming? I see “VariantB” in the doc but we should standardize.

Ethan Park: we can make it `pricing_exp_2026q1.variant = B` and `pricing_exp_2026q1.cohort = control|treatment`. string values.

LaunchDarkly Bot: Feature flag update: `pricing_exp_2026q1` rules modified by Ethan Park (added targeting attribute `billing_profile == STANDARD`).

Chris Osei: perfect.

Soojin Lee: comms checklist:
- Support macro draft (what changed, where to see usage, how overage is calculated)
- Sales FAQ snippet for objections: “are you counting retries?”, “cache hits billed?”, “streaming partials?”
- Console banner copy for “You’re in a limited pricing test” (internal? or customer-facing?)

Michael Grant isn’t here but legal will likely want us NOT to say “pricing test” to customers unless we’re ready to honor that language. can we keep it internal-only for now?

Nikhil Sharma: agree. no explicit customer banner for Variant B. eligibility is self-serve, but still.

Jordan Blake: then at least internal comms: AEs need to know if a customer asks “why did my included usage change?”

Chris Osei: last part: operational checklist for the actual flip:
- [ ] confirm meter_version is pinned for analysis (pre/post)
- [ ] confirm Snowflake export table available to Finance (ledger recon)
- [ ] dashboard links shared in #product + #finance
- [ ] Support macro published
- [ ] Sales enablement FAQ updated
- [ ] set ramp schedule + owner on-call

who’s on point during ramp day 1?

Nikhil Sharma: I can own product side. Logan for telemetry. Aisha for dashboards/alerts. Rishi for finance check.

Logan Wright: ok. pls give 30 min heads up before 5% so we can watch dupes + nulls.

Aisha Bello: i’ll set alert thresholds lower during the first 2 hours so we catch issues quickly, then revert.

Rishi Malhotra: and please freeze any meter changes during the ramp window. don’t want confounding.

Nikhil Sharma: agreed. change freeze: no metering PRs to prod for 48h after first exposure.

Chris Osei: awesome. i’ll post the checklist as a pinned note in this channel + link the Linear ticket (PM-1848/PM-1860) for traceability.

Jordan Blake: :thumbsup: add me to review the Support/Sales wording before it goes out.

text:
Chris Osei: rolling up a rollout checklist for pricing experiment Variant B. want to run it by folks before we flip anything. can we confirm: eligibility, flags, dashboards, comms?

Nikhil Sharma: yes pls. Variant B is the “new free tier + overage guardrail copy” right? + tweak to overage rate for self-serve?

Chris Osei: yep. Variant A was copy-only, Variant B is copy + packaging thresholds (included usage + overage behavior).

Sana Farid: reminder: if meters changed last week, we need explicit pre/post segmentation in the analysis. for rollout checklist: record meter_version and experiment cohort on every request.

Logan Wright: +1. from telemetry side we can tag `meter_version` + `pricing_experiment_variant`. PR-27533 adds cohort tagging but we need to ensure it’s wired on OpenAI-compat + SDK retries.

Monica Patel: SDK side: python/ts will pass idempotency keys by default on retries, but older clients may not. for the experiment cohort tagging, can we do it server-side based on account_id so it’s not client-dependent?

Nikhil Sharma: cohort assignment should be server-side (control plane) based on account + workspace. client should be unaware.

Chris Osei: ok draft checklist items (pls correct):
1) Eligibility:
- self-serve only (no sales-led contracts)
- Hosted API only (exclude Dedicated + Private)
- exclude accounts with custom rate cards / invoice terms
- exclude accounts with open billing disputes (Support tag)
- geo: start US/EU only?
2) Flags:
- LaunchDarkly: `pricing_exp_2026q1` + `pricing_exp_variant=B`
- ramp: 0% -> 5% (24h) -> 25% (48h) -> 50% if stable
- ensure holdout/control remains 50%
3) Telemetry:
- events: `pricing_plan_exposed`, `plan_id`, `variant`, `cohort_id`
- attach to usage events + billing ledger export (Finance recon)
4) Dashboards:
- conversion funnel, ARPU, gross margin proxy, churn/retention
- guardrail: support tickets, dispute rate, “usage export mismatch” cases
5) Comms:
- Sales/Support FAQ snippet + internal heads-up
- finance: invoice preview note for impacted accounts

Soojin Lee: on comms: if we change overage behavior, we need consistent copy in Console + docs + emails. otherwise Support gets crushed.

Jordan Blake: sales request: clear “what changed / who is affected / how to answer fairness questions”. esp billed vs raw tokens + caching.

Rishi Malhotra: finance request: for the ramp steps, can we get an “invoice preview” report daily for impacted cohort? need to validate revenue/margin deltas aren’t coming from meter drift.

Aisha Bello: dashboards: I can add an annotation stream when ramp changes (5%/25%/50%) so we can correlate with metrics + alerts. but need the exact flag key + timestamp.

Ethan Park: eligibility can be enforced at control plane: include rule “account.plan_type == SELF_SERVE” and “account.billing_profile == STANDARD”. we can also block if `billing_hold=true`.

Chris Osei: love it. quick questions:
- US/EU only: do we need region constraint for pricing? or just ensure region dimension is correct for margin attribution.

Logan Wright: pricing calc shouldn’t be region dependent but margin model is. we had the “missing region dimension” incident recently. i’d prefer we limit to accounts where region is stable + the region dimension is present (null-rate < threshold) for the first ramp.

Sana Farid: +1. for experimental validity: excluding accounts with missing region may bias, but for rollout safety it’s fine. just document that exclusion.

Nikhil Sharma: ok, include a “data quality eligibility”: only accounts with >= 99.5% non-null `region` on usage events in last 7d.

Chris Osei: adding.

Soojin Lee: also: console surfaces “included tokens” and “overage starts at”. need to ensure the UI strings are behind the same flag, otherwise ppl see mismatch vs invoice.

Monica Patel: and API/SDK usage endpoint needs to show the same fields (billed_tokens + cache_hit) so customers can reconcile. is PR-27485 already in prod?

Logan Wright: PR-27485 is staged, rolling out to prod this week. but experiment B shouldn’t depend on it being customer-visible—just helps support.

Support Bot: FYI Support queue label `billing-usage-discrepancy` currently at 14 open tickets (last 7d). Top issue: “invoice tokens don’t match usage export”.

Jordan Blake: that’s exactly why we need comms in advance. don’t want the experiment to look like it increased disputes when it’s just confusion.

Rishi Malhotra: on ramp: can we add stop criteria: dispute rate > X%, or gross margin delta outside expected band, or meter drift alerts firing.

Sana Farid: propose stop criteria for first 48h:
- dispute/support tickets in exposed cohort > control by +30% (normalized by active accounts)
- conversion drop > 10% vs control (new signups)
- revenue per active account change > +/-20% without corresponding usage shift
- meter drift alert triggered (ledger vs usage events) beyond threshold

Aisha Bello: we can pipe the drift alert signal into the experiment dashboard as a red banner.

Nikhil Sharma: ok.

Chris Osei: flag + cohort details: do we have final naming? I see “VariantB” in the doc but we should standardize.

Ethan Park: we can make it `pricing_exp_2026q1.variant = B` and `pricing_exp_2026q1.cohort = control|treatment`. string values.

LaunchDarkly Bot: Feature flag update: `pricing_exp_2026q1` rules modified by Ethan Park (added targeting attribute `billing_profile == STANDARD`).

Chris Osei: perfect.

Soojin Lee: comms checklist:
- Support macro draft (what changed, where to see usage, how overage is calculated)
- Sales FAQ snippet for objections: “are you counting retries?”, “cache hits billed?”, “streaming partials?”
- Console banner copy for “You’re in a limited pricing test” (internal? or customer-facing?)

Michael Grant isn’t here but legal will likely want us NOT to say “pricing test” to customers unless we’re ready to honor that language. can we keep it internal-only for now?

Nikhil Sharma: agree. no explicit customer banner for Variant B. eligibility is self-serve, but still.

Jordan Blake: then at least internal comms: AEs need to know if a customer asks “why did my included usage change?”

Chris Osei: last part: operational checklist for the actual flip:
- [ ] confirm meter_version is pinned for analysis (pre/post)
- [ ] confirm Snowflake export table available to Finance (ledger recon)
- [ ] dashboard links shared in #product + #finance
- [ ] Support macro published
- [ ] Sales enablement FAQ updated
- [ ] set ramp schedule + owner on-call

who’s on point during ramp day 1?

Nikhil Sharma: I can own product side. Logan for telemetry. Aisha for dashboards/alerts. Rishi for finance check.

Logan Wright: ok. pls give 30 min heads up before 5% so we can watch dupes + nulls.

Aisha Bello: i’ll set alert thresholds lower during the first 2 hours so we catch issues quickly, then revert.

Rishi Malhotra: and please freeze any meter changes during the ramp window. don’t want confounding.

Nikhil Sharma: agreed. change freeze: no metering PRs to prod for 48h after first exposure.

Chris Osei: awesome. i’ll post the checklist as a pinned note in this channel + link the Linear ticket (PM-1848/PM-1860) for traceability.

title: #product: 1769954321
preview:
Alex Chen: Quick sync — we need a concrete spec for an introspection/discovery endpoint that lists runtime-supported features per model instance (tool schemas, streaming modes, token caps). Customer asks from FinReg want deterministic max token limits and function-calling schemas.
Priya Nair: +1. Proposal: /v1/models/:id/discover returning compact JSON with keys: supported_tools, streaming_modes, max_input_tokens, max_output_tokens, structured_output_schema, function_schemas, version, compatibility. Keep it stable.
Sam Ortiz: Keep payload small and flag-driven. Instead of heavy nested objects, expose top-level capability flags: supports_functions, supports_streaming, streaming_type, supports_json_output, supports_rerank. Put full schemas behind separate endpoints if needed.
Jordan Blake: Also include quantization/embedding caveats — e.g., "embedding_precision": "fp16|int8|approx" to signal deterministic behavior differences.
Maria Gomez: Launch criteria checklist suggestion: contract locked + docs, SDK updated, canary rollout path, perf SLOs validated, legal signoff for token limits for regulated customers.
Alex Chen: Example discovery response (for discussion):
```
{
  "model_id": "redwood-1.4-quant",
  "version": "2026-03-01",
  "capabilities": {
    "supports_streaming": true,
    "streaming_type": "delta",
    "supports_functions": true,
    "supports_structured_output": "json_schema",
    "supports_rerank": "batched",
    "max_input_tokens": 8192,
    "max_output_tokens": 4096,
    "embedding_precision": "int8_approx",
    "supported_tools": ["search","sql-exec"]
  },
  "function_schemas_url": "/v1/models/redwood-1.4-quant/functions",
  "compatibility": {"fallback_models": ["redwood-1.3-std"]},
  "meta": {"cost_estimate_per_1k_output_tokens": "0.012_usd", "p50_latency_ms": "45", "p95_latency_ms": "180"}
}
```
Priya Nair: A few notes: 1) function_schemas_url keeps discovery light-weight; 2) add a TTL header and encourage SDK caching; 3) version the endpoint as /discover?v=1 or /discover/1.
Sam Ortiz: Ops ask: include a signal whether the model enforces strict JSON schema validation vs best-effort post-processing (boolean or enum). Also a deprecation field for soon-to-be-removed features.
Jordan Blake: For streaming, indicate whether tool-calls can stream function arguments (some runtimes block streaming for function call bodies). Field: "function_call_streaming": true/false.
Maria Gomez: Prioritization input: three enterprise pilots require deterministic token ceilings + function schema validation for audit logs. That makes this P0 for Dedicated/Private; for Hosted API we can stage P1 behind perf validation.
Alex Chen: Rollout plan draft: 1) spec and OpenAPI fragment (eng + devex) 2) alpha with 1 partner (dedicated) 3) canary in dedicated pool + SDK flag 4) open-hosted rollout when perf & cost numbers validated. Also add migration docs for SDKs to cache discovery.
deploy-bot: deploy:discovery-schema v0.1 -> staging passed smoke tests :rocket:
Priya Nair: Next steps — I'll open a GH issue with the OpenAPI fragment and owners. Need engineering estimates and a short UX doc for console feature flag.
Sam Ortiz: Rough ETA: spec + tests 3w, implement 4w, docs + SDK updates 1w. Might overlap.
Alex Chen: Thanks, team — sync Thurs 11am to finalize scope and owners. :thumbsup:

title: #product: 1769802467
preview:
Asha Patel: Quick pass — I posted the company OKR draft in Drive. Need a final sign-off by EOD Friday. Two open decisions I want eyes on: 1) launch criteria for Redwood Dedicated SLAs and 2) priority/scope for Redwood Optimize (customer requests + cost tooling). TL;DR proposal: narrow Optimize to beta customers to keep timeline, and set a realistic Dedicated latency target. Please chime.
Tom Reynolds: Saw it — thanks. I agree we should trim Optimize scope. One ask: the Dedicated latency SLO in the doc is 50ms p95 for Q3; that feels optimistic. Suggest we move the committed target to 75ms p95 and keep 50ms as a stretch.
Marta Liu: concur on scope trim. For the SLA bump: we can meet 75ms p95 if we do two infra tasks first — KV cache tuning and kernel fallback paths. Rough ETA ~6 weeks of focused infra work. Hitting 50ms p95 would need an additional senior infra hire or deprioritizing another Q3 project.
Ben Carter: We shouldn't abandon 50ms as our engineering north star, but I support setting 75ms p95 as the committed OKR target and listing 50ms as stretch. Also fine to scope Optimize to beta customers for Q3 to de-risk launch.
Asha Patel: Noted. There is a specific customer ask from Customer X for per-request cost breakdown in the Console. Is that realistic for Q3, or should we scope it down? @Marta — ballpark?
Marta Liu: Per-request / per-token breakouts need token attribution plumbing + events pipeline. If we scope to route-level cost summaries (top-level routes only), that's ~3 weeks. Full per-token attribution is 6-8 weeks and touches billing + analytics.
Tom Reynolds: Proposal: include simplified 'route-level cost breakdown' in the Q3 OKR to satisfy Sales and treat per-token attribution as post-launch work. That should hit the ask without blowing the timeline.
Ben Carter: Agreed. Also flagging a dependency: Dedicated GA must be gated on Compliance Package 2.0 (customer-audited). @ops — timeline?
ops-bot: Compliance package 2.0 deployment windows: staging -> 2024-11-15, prod -> 2024-12-01. Automated schedule update.
Asha Patel: OK — I'll update the doc: 1) Optimize scope -> 'beta customers' only, 2) Dedicated latency SLO -> 75ms p95 (stretch 50ms), 3) Console -> 'route-level cost breakdown' in Q3, 4) per-token attribution -> follow-up item, 5) mark Compliance 2.0 as blocking Dedicated GA. I'll circulate the updated doc for final sign-off by Friday 5pm PT.
Tom Reynolds: Product +1. Quick ask: if anyone has objections, drop them here by EOD Thu so we can lock on Friday.
Marta Liu: Engineering +1.
Ben Carter: CTO +1.
